And I think the main thing to just to to be aware of in those situations is like to make sure that you're reading what the AI agent's updating, you're updating the story dev notes with what's happening so that you know every time like if it goes around in circles and it doesn't figure it out. You want to make sure you're either undoing those changes, but more so you're just documenting what happened so that when you clear the chat, you start again, it knows what it's it's done so it doesn't hallucinate and then start creating duplicate things. Because when you when you go around in circles like that, that's when spaghetti code starts to come out. The AI starts going crazy, adding all these different things to try to solve problems and then forgetting it's done it in the new conversation. So yeah, that's the main thing to be aware of there. Title: The Future of Coding ( BMAD Method ) , Date: Oct 09, 2025
Transcription:
Welcome to this video. What I'm going to be talking about in this video is a framework called BMAD. And I'm going to be jumping behind the scenes and actually talking you through what this is and why this completely levels up your AI coding workflow. So traditional level one AI coding builders are tools like Lovable and Vzero. They're great tools. You can prototype relatively low complex applications on there pretty well, but as soon as you start to step up the complexity, you tend to hit some rabbit holes and some issues building more complicated apps. And so what I recommend for people who are trying to really build anything that's above that real core basic level of application is to dive into AI coding frameworks like the BMAD method. These are slightly slower in terms of development than tools like Lovable, but they actually end up being quicker because they're a hell of a lot more uh efficient. The documentation process is much better and the the actual agent process of building these applications is a lot more controlled and you actually understand what you're doing. So, what I'm going to do in this video is I'm first going to quickly show you through the BMAD method what it is and how it works. And then I'm going to go across to an actual application and show you what it is in practice. paying specific attention to context injection because the main benefit of this framework over tools like Lovable and V 0ero is being able to control the context injection and you can actually modify the configuration and even understand how the configuration works to tailor it to your application and configuring that context injection correctly is how we increase the accuracy of these AI coding methods and reduce hallucinations at the end of the day. So before I get to that, what is the BEMO method? Now, you can, it's completely free and open source. You can come to the BMAD website on GitHub. But what a lot of non-technical people are getting wrong is that you still need to understand the fundamentals of what you're doing. You don't need to understand every line by line piece of code, but you do need to understand how tech works and how to use these frameworks to build apps that are secure and scalable. This video is going to be a journey from zero understanding of tech to a very solid foundational understanding of the fundamentals of apps and how they work. By the end of this video, you'll understand exactly why a lot of these nontechnical founders and vibe coders are failing to build anything production grade. And you'll understand the path for you yourself to be able to build production grade apps with AI. So, what we're going to be talking about in this video is five separate points. We're going to talk about the client versus the server. We're going to talk about authentication and security. We're going to talk about databases. We're going to talk about the front end and the back end. And then we're going to talk about AI and how it helps in all of these areas. Now, if this sounds confusing and overly technical, it is, but I'm going to try and explain it as simply as possible, and there's no shortcut. You need to understand what these are and how these work to actually be able to build production grade AI apps. So, first off, we're going to talk about the client and the server. Now, simply put, the client is the interface that users interact with your app. So, it's usually a web browser, whether that's on your phone or on your computer. We're talking Google Chrome, Safari, Firefox, whatever the case may be. And then we have the server, and this is where operations that relate to the actual operation of the app happen. Now, most people run their servers in the cloud, and so it's usually hosted by AWS or Amazon um or Google or Microsoft. Now, as you build, you'll understand a little bit more about this relationship, but through an app, we're constantly sending messages, which are called API requests back and forth between the client and the server to basically exchange data. Basically, how this works is that through your web browser by logging into a platform, you exchange a username and password and login for a key which allows you to access certain data on the server. A really basic example of this is for let's say we've got a time sheeting and payroll application for employees. Each an employee of a company can log into the platform. They can add a time sheet which is a start hour, a finish hour and basically they can send those forms or time sheets to the server to get stored in a database. And at the end of every week, that server will run an operation or a workflow to calculate all of the hours or time sheets submitted in that week and then send them money based on how many hours they've worked in the week. As you can see in this example, the client is specific to each user. They see the time sheet history and the payrolls relevant to them as a person. And the server is where the calculations happen for each of these clients. So all of the sensitive information or access to the time sheets for each of these users is processed on the server. And so that way people can't hack into other people's information. Now that's a simple explanation of the client and the server. And what I'm going to touch on now is authentication and security. If we look at this little diagram, it explains exactly how this works. So when a user opens your platform on the client, they basically set add a username and a password. We send that username and password to the server because we can't run the authentication process on the client because client code is basically exposed and anybody can see it. So anything that's sensitive has to happen on the server. We send the credentials to the server. The server checks whether those credentials are correct and if they are it sends back an access token. Now, this process is called the authentication process. And there's a lot of different ways to do this, but long story short, we get an access token back. And that access token allows us to send more requests to the server and return information that that key can access. So, if you think about it in really simple terms, that access token is just a key and it can unlock certain doors or certain pieces of data in this database, but there's certain doors that might not want to be unlocked by that key. So, they can't. And a common example for that is in this time sheet and payroll app. Let's say for example we have employees and then we have company admins. Company admins want to see the time sheets and time sheet reports for every user in their company. But they don't want the employees seeing the time sheet report for other employees. So the key or the access token that we get for employees allows them to only access data from the server and the database that's relevant to them. But if a company admin logs in that access token or that key can unlock more doors and it can unlock the doors for every employee to see the total time sheet reports across the entire company for every employee. So long story short that is how authorization works. Now that leads us into the next bit which is databases. Now as you can see in this diagram all of our data is stored in a database. Now we've got two kinds of databases. We've got SQL and NoSQL databases. In simple terms, SQL databases work the same as Excel tables in that we have a number of different sheets. Each of those sheets is a different data type. And in those data types, we have headings or column headers. And then we have rows. Now, really simply, let's imagine users. We have user tables. So, we might have first name as a column, last name as a column, and then user ID as a column, and maybe email and profile image. And then we have a entire list of the users in our application. Now if we want to create a relational database, let's say for example in our previous example, we have users attached to time sheets. So in the time sheet record, one of the columns could be the user ID and that's how we link the user to the time sheet. And so if we wanted to run a search for users, maybe a history tab to see every time sheet they'd ever added, we run a query to the database that goes fetch all of the time sheets in the time sheet table that have the user ID that equals this current user. So that's how SQL databases work. The other option is no SQL databases. And basically what this means is that it's not a tabular structure. So one example of this is documents. So if we wanted to possibly store PDF documents and or just documents in general and each of those documents have different headings. So in the SQL database in our example of users, if we had a user that maybe didn't have a profile image, that column would still exist in an Excel. If you think about an Excel table, the columns always exist. They don't disappear. With a NoSQL database, I mean, there's lots of different ways that this can look, but typically for a document-based NoSQL database, the column headers for each row can be different. So, we could have one user that has a first name, a last name, and an email in our document NoSQL database. The next user could have first name, last name, email, address, phone number, etc. It allows us to store data in a different way. Now, the last thing I'll talk about is vector databases. If you want to learn more about this, I've got a separate video that explains exactly how that works. And these are really popular right now because they it's a new way of quering data and it's very AI friendly. But long story short, this allows us to query data in a cartisian plane by plotting points in a cartisian plane and drawing lines between data to find the shortest distances and pulling information that way. Now, finally, we're up to the front end and the back end, and it kind of links everything that we've talked about here together. is very relevant to this little chart here. But when we talk about the front end, we talk about everything that's rendered in the browser or the client and everything that you see. So when you see dashboards, when you see tables, they're all front-end components and they're compiled together in a combination of three coding languages. So when we talk about HTML, that basically just refers to the structure on the page, what colors, where everything's organized. We have CSS, which is the styles, so fonts, colors, etc. Then we have JavaScript which is basic functioning. So when you click a button where do you go? Which page do you go to? You know when you scroll down what happens? Animations all of that kind of stuff. So when we talk about that we talk about the front end. When we talk about the back end we talk about operations on data. So for example in our previous the previous example we're using the end of every week we need to compile every single time sheet that's been logged for each user. We need to build a time sheet report that has a breakdown of all of the hours they've worked, how much we owe them based on that user's hourly rate. Um, and we want to convert that into a PDF that then gets automatically emailed to every person in the company. Those are backend operations and they happen on the server. So, every app is a combination of front-end code and backend code. Front-end code is exposed in the client. So, we don't want to run any sensitive operations in the front end. And the front end happens on the browser. It's it's code that's rendered and happens in the browser. So, it's in Google Chrome, in Safari, on our device locally. Backend code runs on the server and that's typically hosted in the cloud, but it can also be you can actually run a server on your own computer as well. You've got authentication set up so that only certain users are that users are basically retrieving the data that they should be retrieving. We have what are called vector databases and then we have what's called knowledge graphs. Now long story short the way these work is it's a really smart system for indexing data and indexing the relationships between data so we can search and query them really really quickly and these AI models can actually understand them. Now, the first one's vector databases. And what that basically does is it takes all of your data, it converts it into really long vectors, which is just a a an array of numbers. And it means that we can basically locate your data really, really quickly. So, the way it used to work is you had giant tables. You can think of it as just Excel sheets that had thousands of rows and you had those for invoices, users, expenses, bills, transactions, whatever, proposals, the list goes on. And so searching that data was really intensive. It was slow. And the AI models, they can't really do that that easily at this point in time. So this vectorization approach means that your data becomes a hell of a lot more accessible. And these AI models can read it. But the other part of that technology is what we call knowledge graphs. And what these do is they store the relationship between things in your database. So if you have a client, that client is linked to a number of projects, possibly they're linked to a number of meetings. um they're linked to communication data, they might be linked to some proposal documents, um they might be linked to some ad copies and whatever uh lead channel they came through. And so it stores all of these relationships so that when you interact or when these AI models interact with it, it understands the relationship between everything in your business. It understands that there's certain team members that are assigned to certain projects. There's certain communications that are assigned to a certain ticket or certain comments assigned to a certain ticket in your CRM. All these relationships are stored with this technology. The combination of these two builds a really effective AI brain that we that's what we call it underneath your business that actually understands everything that's happening. And to be honest, it's bigger than any human brain ever will be. Now, this technology is crazy, but almost nobody's using it. And the reason for that is because there's a number of challenges that you need to overcome to adopt this, and I'm going to talk about them quickly. Now, challenge number one is ease of data access. Now, depending on what kind of business you are, your data might still be written on paper. It might be stuck in Google drives or maybe local computer folders, or it might be stretched out across 10 to 20 different tools. You've got CRM tools, document signing tools, project management tools, whatever the case is. That data could be everywhere. And so, to be able to aggregate that into a system where we can name build an AI brain is difficult. And there's a number of different steps that you may need to take to be able to adopt an approach like this. The next step after you've got that sorted is you need to understand how to sync that into this AI brain and how to actually get that set up. And there there's some technical knowledge required for that. And then the third step of that is to analyze your business or your business life cycle every step from client acquisition or all the way through to project or service delivery. And you really need to first have an understanding of that and then be able to have the experience and the knowledge of these AI agents to inspect every uh process in your business and identify areas of opportunity where we can then inject some sort of AI agent to essentially help your business out by reducing manual hours or wasted time and improving the output and the in turn saving you money in each of those steps. So that's a three-step approach to adopting this and there's very few people with the skill set and also the understanding to actually do this right now. But I can almost guarantee that every successful business in 5 years will have recognized this and have adopted this methodology or this process into the way that business works. Just some of the effects that this is going to have on businesses. I'm going to look at each department step by step. So when we talk about sales, we talk about AI automations that can scan all of your meetings, automatically create action items, build summaries, qualify leads based on your ideal buyer personas, have access to 50 plus data points to be able to to qualify these leads. Then go ahead and build scope documents, proposals, documentation, whatever the case is, automatically trigger events, all sorts of things. So there's a lot of hours that can be saved there. And it can also increase the efficiency of whatever human staff that you have in your sales department by 10 times at the minimum. Next, we've got accounting. So, opportunities with accounting include automatic payroll calculations, automatic, you know, tracking all of your finances, automatically building taxation documents, automatically identifying possible leakages or areas of financial opportunity. A lot of accounting, which is really just maths in a lot of scenarios, can also do things like scan government uh schemers and possibly identify tax benefits or whatever the case is. There's a lot of opportunity for AI agents in the accounting space. Next up, we've got marketing. Lot of opportunity here. You can automatically build content uh video content, image content that looks 100% realistic. We can automatically schedule those on through various advertising platforms or through social media and natural posting. We can analyze the analytics from all of those all of those marketing strategies um and then have AI automatically focus on winning strategies. We can train it on viral techniques. Uh so heaps of opportunity in the marketing space and that's one space that's already seeing a lot of shaking up and businesses are already taking advantage of AI to some degree in that space but there's a lot further to go. The final one I'll touch on is support. So support, which is a massive pain point for a lot of businesses because it's not directly connected to revenue, but having five star reviews and a good support system is really important. We can have a support system and a brain that genuinely grows every time it solves a new problem. So it's getting trained on data. It's already trained on all of your business data. If you're a tech company, you could even train it on your on your actual tech stack. Uh whatever business you run that's constantly getting better. It's automatically answering requests for clients. Title: Why Most AI Freelancers Fail (And How to Avoid It) , Date: Jul 29, 2025
Transcription:
If you're freelancing with AI and you're not making money, then you're probably doing one of these things wrong. I've made over $100,000 on Upwork and I've done over 50 projects and I've scaled my hourly rate from $0, literally rock bottom, to over $100 an hour. So, I know a lot of things about how to succeed in the freelancing space. But, I made a ton of mistakes at the start. There's honestly things that I've even learned in just the last three or six months alone that will save you a lot of headaches, a lot of stress, and it's going to let you scale a hell of a lot quicker. First off, I'm going to talk through some of the common freelance failure patterns that I see not only in new freelancers, but also freelancers that have kind of plateaued at a single hourly rate or they're earning the same amount of money week on week and struggling to find out how they can actually grow that. Now, one of the areas that I think that this can be really damaging is fixedpric projects if you don't know how to sell it correctly. Now, one of the dangers and you will learn this the hard way is that every client has different expectations and scope creep especially in the land of AI and tech is very common and it takes extreme expertise and honestly I think a fair bit of luck to actually scope fixed price projects out correctly. Now, the way most people do this is they actually factor in a 30 to 40 to 50% risk factor into that fixed price. So, you'd estimate a total amount of time it takes to complete a project and then you would add the risk factor on top of that because clients at the end of the day, you are really dependent on getting a good review out of the client. So, often you have to go a little bit above and beyond and that can really creep into your profit margins or your profits in general because you're a freelancer. So you have to be really careful of fixed blast projects and you have to be able to scope them out correctly which requires some technical expertise. Now if you don't like that idea, I would always recommend using a more hourly based contract approach. So that is one danger zone. Now, I honestly think that especially as just starting out, you have to do some fixed price projects because they're sometimes a little bit easier to win because the client feels less at risk paying a fixed price because they know exactly how much they're going to be spending. Once you have a little bit more experience and a little bit more value and leverage, you can start to explain to clients that if you're pretty good at a particular thing that you need a 10-hour a week or 20our week retainer and because of the difficulty in scoping this AI solution and how agile tech development is and how quickly AI is changing, it's really difficult to scope out a project. But if I can prove to you what I can deliver in 10 hours and you're happy with that, you get an understanding of how I work, how much I can deliver in a set period of time, and then you kind of negotiate a retainer model, which I honestly think is life-changing. Now, the next one is not knowing how to sell. Now, the weird thing about your hourly rate is it's actually perceived value and not real value. And what I mean by that is that when you get on a meeting with a client, the whole thing is trying to convince them that you're worth how much you say you're worth. Now, you could actually be absolutely horrible at what you do. But if you have a really, really solid social proof, if you talk really, really well, if now it can be difficult to fake this if you don't know what you're doing, but if you can answer really domain, like by domain expertise, I mean the area that you're talking about, but in the AI space, if you can answer complex problems and display examples of solutions that you've had, that's what defines your value. So, if you're really really good at what you do, but you're bad at showcasing it and you're bad at explaining it, you're going to struggle to sell yourself at a high hourly rate because clients aren't that confident that you know what you're doing. The vice versa also applies. If you're not that good at what you're doing, but you're really good at selling yourself, you've organized a lot of social proof, so you've organized a lot of reviews, your profile has a lot of reviews on it or maybe a landing page, um, you've got a bunch of videos and you can showcase examples of projects to clients, you might be able to negotiate a pretty high hourly rate. So, it's really important to understand that value is actually perceived. It's not real. It's how well that you can explain to somebody that you know what you're doing. Now, one of the other ones is undervalued projects. And this one's pretty self-explanatory, but especially if you're doing new projects or you're a new freelancer and you're not 100% sure exactly what's going to be required to complete a project, you may make the mistake of thinking that it's a lot easier than it actually is. Now, if you're starting out, that's not a bad thing because whilst you may not make that much money, you will learn some new skills and you'll learn how to do something new. And I think that actually as when you're starting out, that is almost equally as valuable to money because on a small scale, you can learn something new. So, next time you go to develop a project, you have this new skill that you can sell. But if you're a bigger freelancer, you obviously want to be aware of this issue. And honestly, it's one that kind of teaches itself to you because you'll make this mistake as a freelancer. But you do just want to be careful that you're not undervaluing projects and as your hourly rate grows and as your experience grows that you're reflecting that in your project prices and you're not undervaluing it. The last one, and I actually think this is a really important one that nobody really talks about, is over complicating MVPs. Now, if you're not sure what an MVP is, some people could call it a proof of concept as well or a PC. Essentially, it stands for the minimum viable product. And in AI development, it would be the absolute core product or service or chat or whatever you want to call it. The core project that's required to solve the client's problem. So, if they want a chatbot, for example, that searches their knowledge base. The MVP is just a working chatbot that maybe does their initial 10 20 documents or something like that, then the next stage on top of that could be to add an extra 50 documents. And the next stage on top of that could be to integrate that into their Slack channel or something like that. But what's really important is instead of trying to tackle 10 complex problems at once, you want to break it down into a proof of concept. And there's multiple benefits for this. One, you can deliver it much quicker. Two, it's actually cheaper for the client because they're paying for less things. And three, it reduces the risk of scope creep because the scope is smaller. And it gives you a chance to really quickly display what you can do. So, what I usually recommend is pressuring the client into reducing their scope if they're open to considering it, and they usually are because you're the expert and that's why they're coming to you to basically reduce some of the requirements in the project, deliver them more core product, and that's going to allow you to basically reduce the risk of complexity getting out of hand and you getting stuck in the wasteland of never being able to actually deliver something. Now, those are some of the common common failures. Some of the common successes or the things that I think work really well are one, I've actually found this personally and I've only discovered this in the last three or six months is spritbased pricing. Now, I truly believe that in the tech space, fixbased pricing doesn't actually work. And I've done a lot of fixed based pricing in my time and it can work if you significantly over quote. But I honestly think and it really depends on your business model. I mean the I can definitely see a use case where fixed price pricing could work but we switched to sprintbased pricing and that has been a hell of a lot easier. Client expectations have been way better. We move a lot quicker. We're so much more agile when we're developing AI solutions for clients. And in my personal opinion, I would highly recommend people to look into sprintbased pricing. explore how that works and possibly move over from fixed based pricing because I know a lot of especially freelancers and agencies have serious frustration with fixed based pricing and for me the movement to sprint based pricing has been a no-brainer. It is way way way way better. We're making more money. We're delivering things way quicker. I could not recommend it highly enough. Now the other things are constantly developing your social proof. Now again what I talked about before value is always perceived and the way people charge for high ticket services is basically through perceived value. Building lots of social proof, building a really solid landing page, building videos of client testimonials because videos are such a powerful way to explain how you communicate but also showcase your service and through doing things like building a YouTube channel and demonstrating your skills. And I'm going to shamelessly plug the {YOUR_FREE_COMMUNITY} community right now cuz it's probably a good time. But that's a spot where I showcase my skills in not only freelancing and how to develop and sell yourself, but also how to actually get the technical skills to not only be able to deliver projects in the AI space, which is booming, but also how to accurately quote projects and understand what you're doing and basically either take control of your own freelance career or even maybe develop an agency one day. Now, the other real success pattern, and honestly, this is for really developed freelancers or agencies, is what they call the productized service. Now, when you start out, it's the same kind of concept as beggars can't be choosers. When you're really starting out, you kind of have to take whatever projects come your way because you're in the early stage of your freelancing career and you don't really have the experience or the social proof or the developed presence to be able to turn clients away because you need the money, you need the projects. One thing you'll notice is the problem with that is that you end up taking on all different kinds of projects and you solve lots of different complex problems for each of these projects. So you might build AI solutions for a client in the real estate space for example, but then when you go to build an AI solution for a client in the construction space is completely different. And what I what I'm talking about here is one issue that really plagues people that are trying to scale a really an agency or a freelancing model. And what I'm talking about here is the development of a productized service. So what a productized service means is that you pick one particular painoint or a service and you get really really good at developing that service. So that could be for example building AI chat bots or AI support bots for people in the real estate industry. You get really really good at doing that. And so what you do is you almost build a templated approach or a product to solve that particular problem. And what you've developed is what they call a productized service. And this model means that instead of having to solve really complex problems every time a new client comes through the door, you have a templated approach to solving one particular problem. So as long as you know how to target that particular person that has the problem, you can then obviously deliver a lot more of these projects quicker, but you're still charging the same amount of value. So that's something that I've seen is what takes people from the 10K a month or from a plateau to the next level of freelancing or running an agency. Hopefully that helps you navigate your path wherever you are on the freelancer journey. And this is basically telling your AI chat what to do. Is your assistant possibly a assistant to generate marketing reports? Is it an assistant to generate social media content? Is it an assistant to generate health plans or maybe food plans, diet plans? The list goes on and on. But the instructions essentially tailor that large language model and train it to actually do something purposeful. And then the last thing we have is what's called the knowledge base. And the reason for that is because it actually triggers a web hook, which is basically a notification that you can use to automate things in your business once a meeting transcript has been completed. So, this is my favorite tool of choice. That being said, you can use almost any tool as long as it exposes some sort of API, and we'll talk about that in a little bit more detail, but you can also use Google Workspace's built-in recordings and things like that because what that actually does is it creates a file in your Google folder. And so, what you can see here is it goes receive real-time notifications with web hook events. Now, if you're not familiar with N8N, it's a very similar tool to things like make.com or Zapia, if you've heard of those. They're really common automation tools. The reason we use N8N and not one of the competitors is because of these AI agent modules, which you can see right here. The way these AI agents are built in NAN is really easy to work with. And the main benefits we use it here is because as you can see, it's separated the AI agent into its three categories. And whenever I refer to an AI agent, I say there's three things that build an AI agent. There's your knowledge base, which is the documents or the knowledge that you've provided to the assistant to help it formulate its output. There's the assistant instructions, so what you're actually telling the AI agent to do. And then there's the large language model or the LLM. And as you can see here, it's nice and easily broken down so that if we wanted to possibly change the LLM, let's say for example, a new LLM came out, for example, the whole Deep Seek saga, and you want to quickly change from GPT40 to some DeepSeek model, it's much quicker and easier to do that in NN. And it's really fluid how you can test a bunch of different LLMs. And I really like the way that the AI agents are built, which is why we do it here. Now, let's run an example and actually trigger and set this web hook up. Now, the way you can do this without having to jump on simulated meetings just to set up your web hook is if you go to this uploads field, you can actually upload a video. Keeping note that it's not going to return any participants because it's not a meeting, it's a video. So what you can see on here is we have a number of different metadata and it's really important you get this set up correctly. Now, if you come to here and you come down to the default data loader in NAD, what's really important is to set up your metadata correctly because metadata is what allows filtering in a vector database. So, to quickly explain what happens here, when you upsert a record into a vector database, what you're doing is you're upserting an embedding, which is really a vector that is thousands of numbers long. Now, what's happening behind the scenes at a really high level is they're taking text and they're converting it into this super long list of numbers, which is essentially a numerical way to display a text. And then what they can do is they can do really quick computations to draw similarity searches. And I've made a video on vector databases and how that works. So, if you want to watch that, check out the video above. But at a really high level, what's happening is we're plotting this really, really long list of numbers into this giant database. And what's happening is when you run a search, you're really just looking for the nearest giant number in this massive cartisian space. Now, that might sound really, really confusing. Um, but essentially, it's a much quicker way of doing searches compared to the traditional approach, which was lots of Excel data tables essentially linked together. So, when you wanted to find one record in that database, you'd have to filter across. Let's say there's 200,000 records in your client's database, for example. If you're a massive enterprise, what happens is when you look for one, you have to kind of filter through 200,000 rows to find the row that you're looking for. With a vector database, it's a different way of searching and we're actually just drawing a line from the nearest points to pull data. So, it's a lot quicker and a lot easier to store and search for data. So, what's happening here is we've got an embedding behind the scene, which is this giant number in a cartisian space, but we also have this metadata. And what this is really useful for, for example, the client ID is if I'm on a page and I want to embed a chatbot on our page that allows us to search information just about a client. So let's say I'm in this project page and this project is linked to one client and I want to ask it about what time sheets have been logged against a client or what are the most recent communications that we've had with this client or what are the current pending tasks with this client. What I'll do is I'll make a request to Pine Cone to pull that information, but I'll include a filter for the client ID so that Pine Cone knows not to confuse the search with other clients that we're working with. So, it's a way to kind of chunk up these giant vector databases into categories so that you can filter by a specific client, you can filter by a specific project, you can filter by a specific category. So, that could be communications, it could be time sheets, it could be pro uh proposals, whatever the case is. So, you will want to make sure that you chunk this up in a way that works for your business. What we've done mainly across a lot of these is we've stored the client ID cuz that's our main parent record, I guess, or the the record at the core of this entire lifestyle process. So, it becomes a lead, then it becomes an estimate, then it becomes a project, but at the core of it, it's the same client ID that's underneath all of those different stages. So, we store all of the transcripts and all that kind of stuff against there. But then once it becomes a project or once becomes an estimate, we'll then store those IDs on the record as well in case you want to filter by a client when they just started a project, etc., etc. You will just want to plan out exactly how you set up that metadata to get this to work correctly for you. So to get right into it, let's first start about what is a vector database. So traditionally when we've talked about applications, we typically talk about SQL or NoSQL databases and typically that stores data in either documents or in a tabular format very similar to how Excel works. So the way I typically talk about those kind of databases to nontechnical clients is that your traditional database just looks like a number of different tables those tables have headings and in the tables it's populated exactly like an Excel spreadsheet. So your first row has got your headings for example if you're storing users you'd have maybe first name, last name, date of birth and email and then you just populate that list with all the data. Now, how a vector database works, which is very different to how these traditional database system works, is it works based on similarity searches. Now, I'm going to jump in here and talk about the basics so that you understand what a vector database is. It is more technical than this, and you can dive deeper down the rabbit hole if you want to, but I'm going to talk about the real basics on how this works. So if we think about a regular three-dimensional cartisian plane and if you go back to high school or you know maths class you might have talked about something like this and so we've got an xaxis a yaxis and a z axis and in that three-dimensional space we can plot coordinates onto that cartisian plane or that map if you want to think of it like that. So in this example here, I've plotted three different coordinates. And the way the vector database works is instead of searching through massive tables of data. If you think about an application that has 100,000 users, and we want to find one user when they log in and pull their first name and last name, for example, we have to search through these giant tables to locate that one user. And the way the vector database works is instead of querying information like that, what we do is we query the database with some information. And with that information, we plot a point on a coordinate system like this. And then to find or retrieve information from the database or index is what it's typically called. We draw a line to the nearest coordinate. And that's our response. Now, that's the really basic use case. But you're probably thinking, right, if I want to query users, they're not numbers. So, how are they getting into an index or a database that looks like this? And that's where the technical side of things comes in. So with these LLMs or large language models that are out there these days, so we're talking things like ChatGpt by OpenAI, Anthropic, Llama, Grock, all of the common names that you're hearing out there. What those LLMs usually have is a service called uh embeddings. And what an embedding is is it's a very large what's called a vector. So you'll see this here is a coordinate. And what the embeddings do is they return they return vectors or coordinates that look like this, but have lengths of sometimes they're 1,500 numbers long. One of OpenAI's models returns a vector that's I think 3,76 numbers long from memory. And so what it's doing is it's taking some text input from you similar to how chat GPT works and it's using its this training that it's got to match that text input to a really large number that's thousands of little numbers long and then it's plotting that into a database. So what we're essentially doing is converting natural language into a number. And you're probably wondering how the hell does that work? And to be honest, the LLMs don't really publish exactly how that process works, but I'd assume there's some really deep training to understand. They've probably trained the data on millions of chat messages and millions of articles and all those kind of things to try and understand human language and they call it semantic search or semantic meaning and be able to convert that into a number to accurately draw lines between these giant numbers which really are text and be able to return responses. So that's the behind the scenes on how these vector databases work. It's very useful for taking one-time payments, possibly taking subscription payments, and you can also send payment links and invoices directly out of the platform itself. Some of the common use cases that people are using Stripe for are they may have some sort of SAS application and they want to take onetime payments or subscriptions through that app. They may have some sort of internal tool and they want to create invoices and send payment links out of Stripe or possibly you run some sort of two-sided marketplace where you have sellers and buyers in one platform. So, what I tend to recommend, especially if you're building an MVP or your early stages in your development process, is to try and keep Stripe as lean as possible. So success URL is where the user will be redirected when a purchase or payment or checkout session is successful. Cancel is where they'll go if they cancel at any point. Customer is your Stripe customer ID. Price data is the currency again. Price data name custom payment amount,000 one. So if you're setting up a connect environment, this is the amount that's going to be redirected to you as the service provider. So when a user is first created, we set up this database trigger event so that we actually create use the stripe create customer endpoint with the user's email and name so that we can create them as a customer in your customer section in your Stripe portal. So you get Stripe features set up, OpenAI features set up, chat tasks, forms, all those kind of things that are really common in a lot of applications. So that instead of having to start from scratch on a lot of these apps, hopefully you can modify your style guide. You can drag and drop and change some components. You can delete what you don't think you're going to use in your app. And then hopefully you're from zero to 0.8 in a lot of these basic MVPs in the space of just a couple hours rather than taking days to rebuild authentication, rebuild form functionality, rebuild Stripe, all those kind of things. So that's really what we're trying to solve with this template. And again, the other benefit here is that if it's tried and tested, improved on this template, it's also more secure. So, not only do we get to build quicker, but we know that the features work stably because they've worked in a number of apps and we're not rebuilding them every time. We're rebuilding them once, testing them, making sure they work, and then we're duplicating them in every app that we build. So, that's really the purpose of this template. And what really helps is any feedback from you guys on anywhere you think that we may have missed the mark on certain features in here or anything you think might be missing or not documented and explained well enough. I'd love to hear about it. We want to update our documentation and we want to get this template as comprehensive and as usable as possible. But what I also want to quickly show you is how you can actually access the documentation. So if you come down here and you click documentation, you'll be taken to the documentation link which has a number of explanations on what's happening in the app. Now we really want to develop this further and we want to get this really clear. So anything in here that you think isn't explained well enough or should really have more explanation, I really want to hear about it. explain the difference between the two custom states are basically custom values stored on an element that refresh every time the page is refreshed so they only exist for as long as the browser session is active on this page URL parameters are a little bit different those are parameters that are stored in the URL so when you direct the user to a page and refresh the page whatever stored in that URL stays there so in this scenario if they were on step two and we stored the step parameter in the URL and they refreshed the page they would load back to step two if we use the custom State and refresh the page they would be redirected back to step one now in this case case because we're not saving the multi-line input and drop- down values to the database and we can't preload them to step two if they refresh the page I'm going to be using custom States now how do we set that up best practice is to always set up custom states to the uppermost parent container in your group unless on certain elements especially in repeating groups they cannot be referred to by elements outside of that repeating group in certain scenarios but if the custom state is attached to the hero they can Title: How to Setup a Recursive Workflow in Bubble.io , Date: Mar 25, 2024
Transcription:
so what we're going to explain here is recursive workflows within bubble so what we have here is we return we we run a serice script action which actually returns a two lists so it has output list one which is a text a list of texts and output list two which is a list of numbers and what you can see here we've just tested this to make sure it's working but what you can see here is the list of X this list of text is passed to the field uh exercise for the create exercise performance workflow and then the Reps which is a list of numbers is passed in under the U the Reps field now what we want to do is set up a recursive workflow where we Loop through each item of the two respective lists now we know from the server script that the lists are the exact same size because of this specific way we set up this JavaScript so you don't need to worry about why that is but essentially we know these two lists are the same size or this won't work now what we do is we specify the count as one and what you can see here is we take a count field as a number and then what we do is we Loop through this so the first time it runs it will have a count of one and we we specify for each item in the list we want to get that first item because this is a first Loop or the item number count and we do that for each step here then when we get to the schedule we reschedule it we then want to Loop through the next item in the list so we add count plus one now what we don't want to do here is what set up what's called a an infinite Loop where this keeps adding one to the count and never stops and what that's going to do is actually blow your workflow units through the roof and you'll pay a lot in server usage because this Loop will run forever and ever and never stop so what we want to do is make sure that if the count is we only want to schedule that next Loop if one exists or the count is less than the list size or the exercise the list of texts count so what this will do is make sure that once the count reaches the size of the list it won't schedule this workflow to run again and it'll stop so that's the purpose of that
